item.content:encoded: Good article. It feels very bare at first but when you realise it's full of hidden text it becomes a lot better imo. I don't particularly understand the relevance of the dead Sword of Christ guy though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can't summarize CCP's promotion of atheism as a result of the Cultural Revolution. They chose atheism ideology far earlier than the Cultural Revolution. They treat atheism as a conclusion of historical materialism, and contant religion to colonialism and western centrism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me it came across as completely disconnected from any actual interpretation of G-d I take seriously so the parts that were supposed to be emotionally confronting just fell flat because the premise was so discordant with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "I kid you not kad, I checked the files and damn. Funniest shit I've ever seen."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this object, because its so melancholic.

item.title: April 01/2020
item.content:encoded: god's dead, this is what killed him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing prior sentiments, I feel like this is too barebones and lacks a broader religious perspective that rubs me a little wrong, even if you had deleted material about other religions. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God's apology is more just than his 'justice'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I wasnt feeling particularly strong about this, until I got to the hidden text. And damn, my opinion went up a hundred times. Good hob

item.title: :v
item.content:encoded: Where did you get that page template

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find myself having to echo previous opinion that this is just too short for what it is… too much is left unsaid.

We get this lovely, intense buildup, a new concept for the fallout of the actions (Iscariot event is a superb idea), an amazing picture… and then it falls flat to me with what feels like a post it note of "Killed God because reasons. Gonna go to Hell now. Dinner's in the fridge, k bye" the scale just clashes with itself in my opinion.

No vote for now in case I missed hidden text or something that rounds this out more

item.title: Re: Seed of an Idea
item.content:encoded: couldn't have said it better myself. the imagery evoked by the white text is truly breathtaking, at least for me. realizing it was there changed the way I looked at the whole piece. at the very least, I'd love to see this expanded on, if not in this article, then in some other fashion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i really like this. image is excellent, terrifying in a way you can't really describe - you just dont want to see his face.

as its own article, its good. However, as a X000, there just isn't enough here. hopefully, you'll expand it with some new tales or skips at some point, because this definitely has promise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wondered if they was a "new prophet", but now there is no mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I don't see it as an omnipotent, monothiestic god. I was originally going to go into Iscariot events a little more, mention how they occur any time any deity or religiously significant being dies. So this would occur if, say, the Dalai Lama died and was somehow never replaced, or if the Japanese sun goddess Amaterasu was killed by IJAMEA.

However, people disliked the technobabble bits, so I toned it down and cut out the explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fundamental mistake a lot of religious fiction writers make, especially ones with a focus on a belief-influenced metaphysics, is writing under the idea that a bastardized mix of Jewish mythology and Roman-Christian philosophy is the only religion practiced by a statistically significant group of people. I'm afraid this isn't any different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have to agree that this feels bare-bones. I love Tactical Theology, but nothing seems to have been done with it. I also like white text, but it's so cryptic, it may as well not be there. This felt like it ended right when it was starting to build to something, and I'm just not sure what I'm supposed to take away from this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree with shaggy that the imagery here is lacking, and the event itself feels underexplored. The picture almost makes up for it, but there's very little exploration of the actual nature of Him and His Angels. Given you drop the wild concept of Tactical Theology so bluntly — a technique I love, by the way — I'm guessing that might be intentional? But the wondrous, metaphysical aspects feel like they deserve more love than this vignette gives. It ends up being… disconnected, certainly. Spread a little too thinly, to the point of being almost mundane. Which, again, I think could well have been the point — it just didn't quite fill me with the sense of mystery I think you were shooting for.

It's hard to know exactly what your aim was with this, and I'm novoting because I still need time to digest it. It's certainly thought-provoking, and bits of it were packed full of genuine emotion. I can very well see myself coming back to this later for a second read, which is a commendation in itself. I just don't think that, whatever this was, it quite worked for me.

Also the white-text was distracting as hell, is a common gimmick, and has essentially zero in-universe justification. Me no likey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup. Artist was very kind and allowed me to use the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely an intriguing read with a very interesting way of displaying who/what the corpse is without a ridiculous amount of extraneous detail. It's nice to see an article that doesn't have to be long and drawn out but still keeps me wanting to read it.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it Cerastes but it was barely there. I was intrigued by the fact that angels were there looking after the corpse but my interest kinda wained towards the end with the note at the end about this random guy from the vatican.

I would have liked to see more about why the angels kept showing up whenever the body is disturbed. Maybe a bit more interaction with the vatican and the Foundation could have kept my interest on this.

Aside from my opinion (sorry if its negative), its a good skip and excellently well executed. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with Conwell. The way you pull this off is well-done, and implies some very fascinating divine processes regarding it and the events occurring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I can't say I am a fan of the stylistic choice of having the note here look handwritten (If it was translated wouldn't it just be a typed out document like the rest of the entry?) this is a fun little mystery with some very chilling implications regarding the divine and the slaying thereof.

While I am a little slow to arrive to this party and cannot comment on the previous version, I can say that this is worth a +1. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hold on, humantropy is the same image source as ●●|●●●●●|●●|●, right?

item.title: God's dead, Jim (2.0)
item.content:encoded: Okay, changed a few things. Hopefully you guys like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this was a cool article (hidden text included) until i got to the end, and the link to mctriss's 001?

i guess maybe i kind of get the connection sorta, but it definitely feels forced to me. there's not enough meat here to justify that. and certainly not enough for an X000.

novote because the concept is still really cool and the anomalous effects are interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally like this a whole lot, and also find it properly unsettling that we can discern so little about this dead deity other than that it was covered up. The picture is also extremely good.
I will say I'm not sure it required the linked 001, but I haven't read all the hidden text yet. I think the entry stands alone fine & is more suited to the mystery theme itself.

item.title: Seed of an Idea
item.content:encoded:
It's gonna be a no-vote from me, and here's why -

I don't know if it's because I myself am intensely religious, but I love this concept. I love it so much, and this line just made me love it even more -

It feels beautiful… dying in the dark. You're living in the light. I did my duty.

So many stories and ideas came to mind when I read this line. This is beautiful, and heartbreaking. It deserves to be expanded, dramatically so. It deserves expansion on the scale of Rituals, or the Montauk House. This idea, I think, is strong enough to be nurtured, and grown, and developed into the best article on the website. To keep it this short would be a shame.

Please, please, please keep working on this. There's so much more here, so much potential. Give it the time it needs to grow. And thank you for writing it.

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Staff Review
item.content:encoded:
Reviewing this per a request on IRC.

+ Crit
- Crit

Any changes observed in 5000's condition should be reported to the Department of Tactical Theology immediately.

In this context, "5000" could refer to the SCP or the outpost.

towards the approximate direction of Greenland.

I wouldn't describe direction this way. I would use a compass direction.

Those who are currently non-religious, but have been so in the past will typically experience a religious reawakening, professing their return to their faith. Religious personnel transported forcefully to SCP-5000's near proximity will experience seizures and remain incoherent until removed.

This feels incongruous because you had just gotten done talking about religious people, then you jump to former religious people, and then back to religious people again. That order doesn't make sense.


In general, I find myself not feeling all that strongly about the piece. The fridge logic fell flat for me since I really only know what akiva radiation is in concept and not really much else. All I could really glean was that a god died and the Vatican covered it up by dropping it off in the arctic. It wasn't a satisfying ending, hence my -1 novote. The new note helped add some more character to this.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to TheMightyMcB or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The hidden text trick is still a good one, but I didn't really get anything from this article. It just feels like ground that's already been tread on too many times.

Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: on a technical level its written just fine, and the image used is a damn good one, but this ultimately leaves me out in the cold as this is much too barebones for my personal liking and fails to appeal to me emotionally as well.

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
[+ Outdated Opinion]
– hide block

This one was definitely a difficult one for me. There were parts of this that tried to sway me both ways and I'm still unsure of my novote to some extent. I appreciated the actual overall concept quite a bit. Short articles are something I enjoy, and I'm not incredibly familiar with this aspect of site lore so I knew enough to get what was going on without having some of the issues other commenters have.

However, there were also a few things I disliked. The first is entirely personal and I don't expect many others to have this issue. I almost never enjoy white/hidden text in article. I understand that's not something that can really be addressed in this as it's one of the main things it stands on, but I find it to be confusing from an in-universe perspective and a pain to access on my phone.

Besides that, I think shaggy hit the nail on the head to some extent. While the imagery was passable for me (possibly partly because of the image, though the description of the gravestone also hit pretty hard), I didn't feel any especially strong emotional connection to any of this. This was in part due to the final stinger of the hidden link not having the impact that I imagine you were aiming for. Simply having an explanation for the messages and anomaly did make me go "ah-ha", though after that it felt slightly lacking. Overall though, the exploration of the mortality of deities and similar beings is an interesting concept, and it was mechanically solid enough that I don't feel it deserves a downvote.

The additional characterisation added in an update to this article is something I consider to have improved the article and I have changed my vote to an upvote. There's now enough here for me to feel some emotion, which wasn't really here in a large enough quantity before.

While I'm a little unsure about why the letter is in a handwritten font, especially as it's been translated from another language, it can be argued that it adds some form of personal touch and isn't something that would affect my vote anyway.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This might work for those who aren't well-versed in this particular aspect of wiki lore, but for me, there isn't a whole lot of intruigue to be had here.

The base idea of your subject is definitely one that's worth pursuing — I'm not against it remotely, but it's too bare-bones for me to truly become invested in it. Short articles work for me when there's something very novel, unique, or interesting to make it stand out; evocative tableaus concentrating on imagery or aiming to evoke a specific emotion are also an excellent way to use brevity to your advantage.

This is falling just short of each of those attributes. It's shining light on an established piece of site lore (one that, again, I would like to see explored), so it's not quite going out there in terms of originality… and, despite having the potential to evoke strong emotions or paint a memorable picture, it gets a touch distracted in detailing supplemental details and political events that don't meaningfully contribute to the moment the mystery is built upon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Away with such a God! Better to have no God, better to set up destiny on one’s own account, better to be a fool, better to be God oneself!

God's dead. But why?

First of all, huge thanks to Philip Ob Rey for allowing me to use one his works for my 5kon image. Check out his website here. http://www.humantropy.com/

Permission
Permission Granted

Thank you to everyone who critted this, like lt flops, akimoto and cimmerian, among countless others I'm probably forgetting. Special thanks to RTME and Kinchtheknifeblade for inspiring parts of this.

While you're here, why not check out my other stuff?

Numbers desired: 5003, 5004, 5998, somewhere else between 5001-5010 if available, 5343

psst, check out the white text.
